questions.question: What is the primary purpose of mood monitoring in CBT?

options.text: To prove that your mood is always bad and nothing helps
To identify patterns, triggers, and effective coping strategies by tracking mood over time
To compete with others to see who has the worst mood
To vent emotions without examining them

questions.explanation: Correct. Mood monitoring creates objective data that reveals patterns you might not notice otherwise: 'My anxiety is always worse in the morning,' 'Social contact improves my mood,' 'I feel better on days I exercise.' This information guides treatment and shows you what actually helps.

questions.question: Why is it important to track mood at the same time(s) each day?

options.text: Because mood never changes throughout the day
To create consistent data that reveals patterns rather than random snapshots
Because tracking at different times is too complicated
To make the process feel more rigid and disciplined

questions.explanation: Yes. If you only track mood when you remember (usually when it's intense), you get a distorted picture. Consistent timing (morning, afternoon, evening) shows you the full range of mood variation and helps identify patterns (time of day, after certain activities, etc.).

questions.question: What additional information is helpful to track alongside mood ratings?

options.text: Only the mood number — context doesn't matter
Activities, thoughts, physical symptoms, and sleep quality
Other people's opinions about your mood

questions.explanation: Correct. Context helps you understand what drives mood changes: 'Anxiety was 80/100 after two cups of coffee and poor sleep,' 'Mood improved after talking to a friend,' 'Negative thought: I'm a failure.' This turns mood monitoring into a diagnostic tool that guides intervention.

questions.question: How can mood monitoring help challenge negative thoughts?

options.text: It can't — mood monitoring and thought work are separate
Data showing mood variability contradicts thoughts like 'I always feel terrible' or 'Nothing helps'
Mood monitoring makes negative thoughts worse by focusing on them
You should stop mood monitoring once you identify a negative thought

questions.explanation: Exactly. When you think 'I'm always depressed,' mood tracking might show your mood ranges from 40-70/100, with higher scores after certain activities. This evidence directly contradicts the all-or-nothing thought and shows you have more control than you believed.

questions.question: If you tracked your mood for the next week (0-100, three times daily), what patterns do you think you might discover? What factors (sleep, exercise, social contact, thoughts) do you predict would correlate with better or worse mood?
